front: What two clauses make up the Fourth Amendment?
back: The reasonableness clause (the general command that searches and seizures be reasonable) and the warrant clause (no warrants shall issue but upon probable cause).

front: Fourth Amendment text: it secures the right of the people to be secure in their {{persons, houses, papers, and effects}}, against unreasonable searches and seizures.
back: persons, houses, papers, and effects
cite: U.S. Const. amend. IV

front: Whom does the Fourth Amendment restrain — and whom does it NOT restrain?
back: It restrains the government only; it does not reach private parties acting on their own initiative.
cite: United States v. Jacobsen, 466 U.S. 109, 113-14 (1984)

front: What is the four-step order in which every Fourth Amendment problem is worked?
back: (1) Was a constitutionally protected area or interest involved; (2) did a search or seizure occur; (3) does the claimant have standing (were the claimant's OWN rights invaded); and (4) was the government action reasonable (C.R.E.W.).

front: In the four-step framework, what question is asked at the STANDING step (step 3)?
back: Were the claimant's OWN protected interests invaded — was the place searched or the thing seized his? Fourth Amendment rights are personal.

front: In the four-step framework, how is the REASONABLENESS step (step 4) operationally satisfied?
back: Through C.R.E.W.: Consent, a Recognized Exception, or a Warrant.

front: What does the instructor mnemonic C.R.E.W. stand for, and what does it operationalize?
back: Consent, Recognized Exception, or Warrant — the three ways government action becomes reasonable. (C.R.E.W. is an instructor maxim, not case law.)

front: What are the two independent definitions of a Fourth Amendment 'search'?
back: (a) The government physically intrudes on a constitutionally protected area to obtain information (trespass theory), OR (b) the government invades a reasonable expectation of privacy (Katz theory). Either independently establishes a search.

front: What does the TRESPASS theory of a search require that the Katz theory does not?
back: A constitutionally protected AREA — persons, houses, papers, or effects — physically intruded upon to obtain information.

front: Is the Katz (reasonable-expectation-of-privacy) theory of a search confined to persons, houses, papers, and effects?
back: No — it protects a reasonable expectation of privacy and is not confined to the enumerated categories: 'the Fourth Amendment protects people, not places.'

front: Did the Katz privacy theory REPLACE the property/trespass baseline for searches?
back: No — Katz supplemented the property baseline; it did not replace it. Either theory independently establishes a search.

front: Define a Fourth Amendment seizure of PROPERTY (Jacobsen).
back: 'Some meaningful interference with an individual's possessory interests in that property.'
cite: United States v. Jacobsen, 466 U.S. at 113

front: Which case defines a property seizure as 'some meaningful interference with an individual's possessory interests in that property'?
back: United States v. Jacobsen, 466 U.S. 109 (1984).
cite: United States v. Jacobsen, 466 U.S. at 113

front: Define a Fourth Amendment seizure of a PERSON.
back: A restraint on liberty of movement by physical force or by submission to a show of authority.

front: Distinguish what is interfered with in a seizure of PROPERTY versus a seizure of a PERSON.
back: Property seizure = meaningful interference with possessory interests in the property. Person seizure = restraint on liberty of movement (by force or submission to a show of authority).

front: What did Camara v. Municipal Court hold about administrative area inspections?
back: Administrative inspections ARE Fourth Amendment searches.
cite: Camara v. Municipal Court, 387 U.S. 523 (1967)

front: Quote Camara's statement of the reasonableness-balancing test.
back: '[T]here can be no ready test for determining reasonableness other than by balancing the need to search against the invasion which the search entails.'
cite: Camara v. Municipal Court, 387 U.S. at 536-37

front: What is the 'touchstone' of the Fourth Amendment, and what is its essential character?
back: Reasonableness is the touchstone, and it is a BALANCE — Camara's balancing of the need to search against the invasion is the root of every later reasonableness inquiry.

front: Why does Camara v. Municipal Court matter to the broader framework (its significance)?
back: Its balancing formulation — need to search vs. the invasion — is the root of every later Fourth Amendment reasonableness inquiry.
cite: Camara v. Municipal Court, 387 U.S. at 536-37

front: What is the core rule of Fourth Amendment standing?
back: Fourth Amendment rights are personal: a defendant may suppress only evidence obtained by invading HIS OWN protected interest, not a third party's.

front: Common pitfall: officers/instructors conflate 'the search was illegal' with what — and why is that wrong?
back: They conflate it with 'THIS defendant can suppress.' Wrong: standing requires the claimant's OWN protected interest to have been invaded, not a third party's.

front: Key facts → case: An officer moved stereo equipment a few inches to read its serial numbers. Which case, and was that a search?
back: Arizona v. Hicks — yes, moving the equipment was a NEW search requiring probable cause.
cite: Arizona v. Hicks, 480 U.S. 321 (1987)

front: What rule does Arizona v. Hicks stand for about the magnitude of an intrusion?
back: A trivial / minimal further intrusion is still a search — even moving an object a few inches to read a serial number requires probable cause.
cite: Arizona v. Hicks, 480 U.S. at 325

front: In Hicks, picking up the turntables to read serial numbers 'produced a {{new invasion}} of respondent's privacy unjustified by the exigent circumstance.'
back: new invasion
cite: Arizona v. Hicks, 480 U.S. at 325

front: Common pitfall (Hicks): what do officers forget about small intrusions?
back: That small intrusions count — moving an object a few inches to read a serial number is itself a search.

front: Why is STATE ACTION described as the threshold for the whole Fourth Amendment?
back: Because the Amendment does not apply at all to a search or seizure (even an arbitrary one) effected by a private party on his own initiative — without government action there is no Fourth Amendment event.
cite: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n, 489 U.S. 602, 614 (1989)

front: Per Skinner, when DOES the Fourth Amendment apply to conduct by a private party?
back: When the private party 'acted as an instrument or agent of the Government' — not when he acted 'on his own initiative.'
cite: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n, 489 U.S. at 614

front: Per Skinner, whether a private party is a government agent 'necessarily turns on' what?
back: 'The degree of the Government's participation in the private party's activities,' resolved 'in light of all the circumstances.'
cite: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n, 489 U.S. at 614-15

front: A private party becomes a state actor when the government does what?
back: When the government instigates, directs, or significantly encourages or participates in the search.

front: What did Coolidge v. New Hampshire establish about evidence from a genuinely private search?
back: Evidence produced by a genuinely private search does not implicate the Fourth Amendment; whether conduct is private or governmental is determined in light of all the circumstances.
cite: Coolidge v. New Hampshire, 403 U.S. 443 (1971)

front: Common pitfall: why is private-party evidence NOT automatically 'clean'?
back: It is clean only if the actor was genuinely private. If the government instigated, directed, or significantly participated, the private party is a state actor and the Amendment applies (Skinner).

front: What is the commonly taught two-part private-agency test, and what is its actual authority status?
back: (1) Government knowledge and acquiescence, and (2) the private party's intent to assist law enforcement. It is CIRCUIT GLOSS — treat it as persuasive, not a SCOTUS holding.

front: State the private-search doctrine ('the cat is out of the bag').
back: Once a private party has searched and exposed contents, a later government inspection that does NOT exceed the scope of the private search invades no remaining expectation of privacy.
cite: United States v. Jacobsen, 466 U.S. at 115-17

front: Key facts → case: FedEx employees opened a damaged package, then the DEA inspected it. Which case, and was the government inspection lawful?
back: United States v. Jacobsen — the DEA's later inspection within the scope of the private search was lawful (invaded no remaining privacy).
cite: United States v. Jacobsen, 466 U.S. at 115-17

front: What are the three sub-rules governing the private-search doctrine?
back: (1) Cannot exceed the private search's scope (Walter); (2) re-examination needs 'virtual certainty' nothing else of significance will be revealed (Jacobsen); (3) the officer must already have a lawful right of access to the item.

front: Private-search sub-rule 1: what happens if the government search exceeds what the private party exposed?
back: It is a SEPARATE search needing its own justification — the government may stand in the private party's shoes but no further (Walter).
cite: Walter v. United States, 447 U.S. 649 (1980)

front: Key facts → case: A private party turned over films it had NOT actually viewed, and the FBI then screened them. Which case, and was the screening lawful?
back: Walter v. United States — the FBI's screening of films the private party had not viewed was a separate, UNLAWFUL search exceeding the private search's scope.
cite: Walter v. United States, 447 U.S. 649 (1980)

front: Quote Walter on what a partial private search does (and does not do) to remaining Fourth Amendment protection.
back: The private 'search merely frustrated that expectation in part. It did not . . . strip the remaining unfrustrated portion . . . of all Fourth Amendment protection.'
cite: Walter v. United States, 447 U.S. at 659

front: Private-search sub-rule 2: when may officers re-examine or reopen what a private party already searched?
back: Where there is 'a virtual certainty that nothing else of significance' would be revealed beyond what the private party already disclosed (Jacobsen).
cite: United States v. Jacobsen, 466 U.S. at 119

front: Private-search sub-rule 3: what access requirement must the officer satisfy?
back: The officer must already have a lawful right of access to the item the private party searched.

front: Common pitfall: what is 'the Walter trap' in private-search cases?
back: Going beyond what the private party exposed — without independent justification or virtual certainty nothing new will appear — is a FRESH search. The officer stands in the private party's shoes but no further.

front: Common pitfall: what happens if you skip the threshold (search-or-seizure) question?
back: If no search or seizure occurred, there is nothing to justify and no suppression remedy. Work the sequence in order; don't jump to warrant exceptions.

front: For what single framing point is District of Columbia v. Heller cited on this Fourth Amendment page (and what caution applies)?
back: Only for the framing that the Bill of Rights was 'widely understood to codify a pre-existing right, rather than to fashion a new one.' CAUTION: Heller is a SECOND Amendment case, NOT Fourth Amendment authority.
cite: District of Columbia v. Heller, 554 U.S. 570, 592 (2008)

front: Which case supplies the privacy ('reasonable expectation of privacy') theory of a search?
back: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347 (1967). (Deep treatment lives on the 'two searches under the 4th Amendment' page.)
cite: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347 (1967)

front: Which case revived the trespass/physical-intrusion theory of a search (GPS tracker on a vehicle)?
back: United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400 (2012). (Deep treatment lives on the 'two searches under the 4th Amendment' page.)
cite: United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400 (2012)

front: Distinction: which search theory is associated with Jones, and which with Katz?
back: Trespass / physical-intrusion theory → Jones. Reasonable-expectation-of-privacy theory → Katz. Each independently establishes a search.
